accepted.question_text: For the disordered asymmetric zero-range process on Z^d with random site rates and a critical density rho* determined by the disorder law, can the hydrodynamic limit be extended to describe the macroscopic behavior when the initial density lies above the critical density rho*? Equivalently, can the high-density hydrodynamic result that is known for the one-dimensional totally asymmetric zero-range process with rate function r(k) = I{k >= 1} be generalized to more general zero-range processes with other bounded monotone jump-rate functions r?

evidence.quote: Problem 1. Extend Theorem 1 to describe hydrodynamic behavior also at high density rho > rho*. Or, equivalently, extend Theorem 2 to more general ZRP's.

The proof of Theorem 2 in Seppalainen and Krug (1998) depends on special properties of the totally asymmetric ZRP with rate function r(k) = I{k >= 1}. The proof of Benjamini et al. for Theorem 1 follows a strategy invented by Rezakhanlou (1991), and may be a better candidate for generalization.

accepted.question_text: In the phase-transition regime of the disordered asymmetric zero-range process, what happens on space scales finer than the hydrodynamic scale when the particle density exceeds the critical density rho*? More specifically, can one rigorously prove and characterize the expected formation of spatial inhomogeneities in which particles concentrate on exceptionally slow sites, rather than exhibiting only the hydrodynamically trivial behavior that the macroscopic profile does not move?

evidence.quote: Problem 2. In the phase transition case, the hydrodynamic theorem reveals only trivial behavior at high density rho > rho* (the profile does not move, see Theorem 2). It is expected that on a finer space scale one can see inhomogeneities develop, specifically, that the particles concentrate on exceptionally slow sites.
No rigorous results exist to decribe these phenomena. Krug (1998) and Seppalainen and Krug (1998) discuss this in terms of the exclusion version of the totally asymmetric ZRP.

accepted.question_text: Fix a realization of the random site rates alpha for the disordered zero-range process, and start the process from a spatially ergodic initial distribution of particle configurations with density rho. Does the law of the time-evolved configuration eta(t) converge weakly, as t tends to infinity, to one of the equilibrium product measures nu^alpha_phi? In the phase-transition case when rho exceeds the critical density rho*, does eta(t) converge weakly instead to the equilibrium whose density is exactly rho*?

evidence.quote: Problem 3. An interesting open problem for the disordered ZRP concerns the weak convergence of the process: Fix the rates alpha, and take the initial distribution of eta(0) spatially ergodic with density rho. Does the distribution of eta(t) converge weakly, as t -> infinity, to one of the equilibria nu^alpha_phi?
If rho > rho* in the phase transition case, does eta(t) converge weakly to the equilibrium with density rho*? For the standard ZRP results of this type were proved by Andjel et al. (1985).

accepted.question_text: For the disordered totally asymmetric simple exclusion process, and more generally for the one-dimensional totally asymmetric K-exclusion process with random rates and hydrodynamic flux function f_K(rho), does there exist a phase transition analogous to the phase transition known for the disordered zero-range process? In particular, does the macroscopic flux function f_K(rho) develop a flat segment on an interval centered around the density rho = K/2?
accepted.context_brief: Phase transition and flux flatness for disordered TASEP

evidence.quote: Problem 4. For the disordered TASEP, any new rigorous results would be welcome. For example, is there a phase transition similar to the one for disordered ZRP? Does the flux function fK(rho) have a flat segment on an interval around rho = K/2?

Theorem 3. Fix a positive integer K and an i.i.d. distribution Q for alpha = (alphax). Then there exists a concave function fK on [0, K] that depends on Q such that, if u(x, t) is the unique entropy solution of (2.18) with flux f = fK, then under assumption (A.5), the limit in (2.19) holds for Q-a.e. alpha, for each t > 0, phi in C0(R), and epsilon > 0.
